metadata.source_title: Leisure again shows improvement in the well-being of staff
Leisure again shows improvement in the well-being of staff

text: The well-being of staff in the leisure sector improved last year, while the average in other sectors deteriorated slightly. Despite this catch-up, the sector still has a lot of catching up to do.

metadata.source_title: Leisure again shows improvement in the well-being of staff

text: Employers should above all limit the workload.

metadata.source_title: Leisure again shows improvement in the well-being of staff

text: While well-being deteriorated in most sectors last year, the leisure sector shows an improvement. This is mainly due to significant progress in safety, equal opportunities and personal development of staff in the catering and leisure sector. In the meantime, the workload remains high and that is also taking its toll on the health of leisure employees. The key to growth of companies in the leisure sector lies largely in the well-being of employees. Satisfied employees are less likely to leave the organization and committed employers are more likely to attract new staff. In addition, higher well-being contributes to higher productivity because satisfied employees are more productive and sick less often. Entrepreneurs in the leisure sector who invest in staff are rewarded with more turnover and higher returns. The fact that well-being in the leisure sector improved last year is mainly due to safety and room for personal development in the workplace. For example, last year almost a quarter of leisure staff indicated that the development of skills is encouraged by their manager. A steady increase: until three years earlier, only one in five employees was given room for further development. Yet there are still opportunities, because leisure has the lowest participation of staff in training and courses compared to other sectors. Another aspect where the leisure sector is showing improvement is satisfaction with working conditions. Since the first measurement in 2015, the share of employees who are satisfied with their working conditions has increased from 70 to 75 percent. In addition, far fewer leisure employees are afraid of losing their jobs and have seen their salaries rise considerably in recent years. Maintain work-life balance. Employers in the leisure sector must especially limit the workload to further improve the well-being of staff. In particular, the balance between work and private life in leisure scores considerably lower than in other sectors, despite an improvement over the years.

metadata.source_title: Leisure again shows improvement in the well-being of staff

text: This is mainly because the work is not flexible and staff must always be physically present. The fact that the workload is high is also evident from the speed at which leisure staff have to carry out work. For example, 40 percent of employees indicate that they often or always have to work quickly. In other sectors this is on average 30 percent of the workforce. The relatively high workload is partly caused by peak times, such as during lunch and dinner, when the demand for staff is highest. The high workload is reflected in the health score of leisure staff. More than 15 percent of employees often feel completely exhausted. Almost a third of employees also often feel empty due to work, while in 2015 only one in five employees regularly experience this feeling. This may also be due to demanding interactions with colleagues and customers. The number of employees who experience the interaction as demanding has doubled since 2015. Another possible explanation for this is the high proportion of young employees in the sector. According to the Hospitality Sector Labor Monitor, the average age is 30.7 years. Work pressure-related complaints appear to be relatively common, especially among young employees: research by the TNO knowledge institute shows that 23 percent of men and 29 percent of women between the ages of 18 and 34 experience such complaints. The staff shortage makes it challenging for employers to reduce the workload of staff. Yet it is important that entrepreneurs make choices to protect and retain their staff.

metadata.source_title: Leisure again shows improvement in the well-being of staff

text: Forecasting revenue offers opportunities by analyzing past revenue patterns, taking into account seasonal trends, cyclical fluctuations and even weather forecasts. This enables companies to schedule employees more intelligently, tailored to the expected workload.

metadata.source_title: Leisure again shows improvement in the well-being of staff

text: A good example of this is Poké Perfect, a chain that uses this effectively.

metadata.source_title: Leisure again shows improvement in the well-being of staff

text: In addition to benefits in personnel management, working with detailed sales forecasts makes it possible to purchase more efficiently and reduce food waste.

metadata.source_title: Leisure again shows improvement in the well-being of staff
Leisure again shows improvement in the well-being of staff

text: Every year, ABN AMRO measures the well-being of staff in all sectors in the Netherlands. ABN AMRO's Sectoral Welfare Monitor is based on predominantly public sources, such as the Central Bureau of Statistics and the Netherlands Organization for Applied Scientific Research (TNO). Separate data from the National Working Conditions Survey (NEA) was also purchased from TNO to make the monitor more complete. Variables that required calculations were added manually.

metadata.source_title: Leisure again shows improvement in the well-being of staff

text:
For more information about the Sectoral Welfare Monitor, [see this publication, page 33 (PDF)](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/media/rapport-sectorale-welzijnsmonitor-augustus-2023_tcm16-205163.pdf) and further. The monitor covers seven main categories (work-life balance, health, personal development, economy, equal opportunities, safety and environment) with 87 underlying variables. Due to a lack of current figures, only five of the main categories (work-life balance, health, personal development, safety and equal opportunities) are included in this publication. These categories contain 71 of the 87 variables.
[Also read our sector analysis from last year](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/sectorprognoses/leisure/groei-spelen-en-toerisme-stutten-Vrijetijdssector.html)
